title: DSL node reference generator
description: Java entrypoint that introspects MeshNodeRegistry_MeshNodes.MAP, instantiates each node, calls inputs() and outputs(), and emits a structured JSON schema per node: {id, sourceFile, inputs: [{name, type, default, modeConstraint}], outputs: [{name, type}]}. Exposed via 'uv run ixdar-cli dsl-node-reference'. Currently 45 annotated nodes. This reference is the foundation for both LSP support and AI-agent context — an agent working on a DSL file can read the reference to know what nodes exist, what ports they have, and what values are valid.
definition-of-done: 1. A Java main class (e.g., ixdar.tools.DslNodeReference) emits JSON to stdout with all registered node definitions. 2. Each node entry includes: id, Java source file path, inputs (name, PortType, default value, mode constraint if any), outputs (name, PortType). 3. The CLI command 'uv run ixdar-cli dsl-node-reference' invokes the Java tool and returns the JSON. 4. The output is valid JSON and includes all 45+ registered nodes. 5. The output is stable (sorted by node id) for diffing.
testing-plan: 1. Run the Java tool, parse the JSON output, verify it contains all registered nodes. 2. Spot-check a few nodes (e.g., float_math, subdivide_mesh, input_position) against their actual Java implementations. 3. Run the CLI command, verify it returns the same JSON.
todos: Iterate MeshNodeRegistry, instantiate each node, extract inputs/outputs
Serialize to JSON with node id, source path, ports, defaults, mode constraints
Add dsl-node-reference command to the Python CLI
Write a test verifying the output covers all registered nodes
unknowns: Whether to invoke the Java tool via mvn exec:java or a pre-built jar
Whether to cache the reference JSON on disk or regenerate each time
